summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that GET /api/settings returns the node.secret UUID in the response JSON. The extracted secret was then used as the X-Node-Secret header to successfully call GET /api/users, confirming full authentication bypass.
expected_security_impact: Calling GET /api/settings should expose the node.secret field. Using this secret as the X-Node-Secret header should grant full admin access, allowing access to any authenticated endpoint.
actual_observed_result: Running the reproduction built Nginx UI v2.3.3, installed it, logged in as admin, and called GET /api/settings. The response included node.secret with value 9ec67bd2-d827-492d-afdf-d45bedf375e9. Using this secret as X-Node-Secret header on GET /api/users returned 200 with user data, confirming full auth bypass.
verification_method: The exploit script extracts node.secret from the settings response and writes it to /tmp/info_disclosure_proof.json. It then attempts to call GET /api/users with the X-Node-Secret header. A 200 response confirms the secret works as an authentication bypass. The proof files are checked for the SECRET_EXPOSED and AUTH_BYPASS_CONFIRMED flags.
evidence_produced_by_run: GET /api/users with X-Node-Secret header returned HTTP 200 with user data
/tmp/info_disclosure_proof.json contains SECRET_EXPOSED: true with the extracted secret
/tmp/auth_bypass_proof.json contains AUTH_BYPASS_CONFIRMED: true with user list data
The generated secret was logged during boot: 'Generated Secret: 0e2757e3-a3d0-4f88-964d-a1c326e9e233' and the install process generated a new secret which was returned in the settings API

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo. The Dockerfile also downloads the pre-built v2.3.3 release binary from GitHub.
